articles.title: Kenyan Runner Sabastian Sawe Breaks Two-Hour Barrier in London Marathon
articles.summary: Sabastian Sawe, a 30-year-old Kenyan athlete, made history at the London Marathon by becoming the first person to run a marathon in under two hours in a competitive race. His time of 1:59:30 shattered the previous world record of 2:00:35 set by Kelvin Kiptum in 2023. Sawe was already on world record pace at the halfway mark (1:00:29) and accelerated in the second half. Debutant Yomif Kejelcha also finished under two hours in 1:59:41, becoming the second man to achieve this feat in race conditions. Half marathon world record holder Jacob Kiplimo finished third in 2:00:28. In the women's race, Ethiopia's Tigst Assefa improved her own world record for a women-only field with a time of 2:15:41. The men's wheelchair race saw Swiss athlete Marcel Hug win his eighth London Marathon title, tying David Weir's record. Catherine Debrunner also retained her women's wheelchair title. Sawe expressed joy and excitement after the race, having told BBC earlier that breaking the record was 'only a matter of time.'

articles.title: UK Braces for Eight Months of Higher Prices After Iran Conflict
articles.summary: The UK government has warned that higher prices for energy, food, and flights could persist for at least eight months after the end of the US-Israel war with Iran. Darren Jones, Chief Secretary to the Prime Minister, stated that the government is actively planning to mitigate potential shortages and economic disruption. The conflict has disrupted energy production and transportation across the Middle East, leading to global supply chain issues and price increases. Officials have prepared worst-case scenarios, including potential shortages of chicken and pork by summer. The government is urging the public not to panic, advising drivers to continue using petrol normally and not to alter travel plans. Jones emphasized that price pressure is more likely than empty shelves. The IMF predicts the UK will be hit hardest among advanced economies, cutting its growth forecast from 1.3% to 0.8%. The government holds contingency meetings twice weekly and is planning for possible disruptions in CO2 supply, essential for food preservation and animal slaughter. The UK remains non-combatant but is taking defensive actions to protect its interests.

articles.title: Trump Rushed to Safety After Gunfire at White House Correspondents' Dinner
articles.summary: During the White House Correspondents' Dinner at the Washington Hilton hotel, gunfire erupted, causing panic. President Donald Trump was swiftly evacuated by Secret Service agents to a secure location. The event, attended by Vice-President JD Vance, Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt, and many politicians and journalists, was disrupted when a suspect opened fire. Police identified the suspect as Cole Tomas Allen, 31, from Torrance, California, who was a guest at the hotel. He was found carrying multiple weapons, including guns and knives. One security agent was injured and taken to the hospital. Authorities are investigating the motive. The incident highlights ongoing concerns about security at high-profile events and the risks faced by public figures.

articles.title: Gunman Arrested at White House Correspondents' Dinner Hotel
articles.summary: On Saturday night, a 31-year-old man named Cole Tomas Allen was arrested after firing shots inside the Washington Hilton hotel, where the White House Correspondents' Dinner was being held. Allen, from Torrance, California, was a guest at the hotel. He was armed with a shotgun, a handgun, and multiple knives. According to law enforcement sources, Allen told officials he intended to shoot members of the Trump administration. At least five to eight gunshots were fired, and security exchanged fire with the suspect. Allen was not hit but was taken to the hospital for evaluation. Police Chief Jeffery Carroll stated that Allen appears to be a lone actor. Allen was previously employed at a tutoring company and graduated from Caltech in 2017. He faces federal charges including using a firearm during a crime of violence and assault on a federal officer. The incident occurred as the annual dinner for journalists and politicians was taking place, but no attendees were harmed.

articles.title: Early Tick Season Raises Concerns Among Doctors
articles.summary: Tick season in the United States appears to be starting earlier and more intensely than usual, with a higher number of bites already reported. This has led some doctors to worry about a potentially severe year for tick-borne diseases, such as Lyme disease. Dr. Alina Filozov, an infectious disease specialist in Connecticut, notes that increased exposure to ticks likely means more infections. The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) issued an early advisory urging the public to take precautions. According to CDC Lyme disease expert Alison Hinckley, data indicates that ticks are active and people are being bitten now, even though tick bites typically peak in May. The CDC's tracking system shows that weekly ER visits for tick bites are at their highest for this time of year since 2017, across all regions except the south-central U.S. However, the data is limited because it only includes hospital visits, not all bites. Researchers will need months to systematically sample tick populations, and it will take time to determine if there is an actual surge in tick-borne illnesses. Not every tick bite leads to infection, but the early signs are concerning.

articles.title: Flight Cancellations Rise Due to Jet Fuel Costs
articles.summary: Airlines worldwide are canceling flights as the war in the Middle East drives up jet fuel prices, and the timing is problematic because summer travel demand is increasing. For travelers, this means navigating a confusing system of passenger protections that vary by airline and country. Fuel-related cancellations are often announced days or weeks in advance, giving passengers more time to adjust plans compared to weather-related cancellations. The article advises passengers to check the airline's app or website for rebooking options, especially on U.S. carriers which have better digital tools. For non-U.S. carriers, it recommends trying multiple channels like customer service lines or airport desks. The article also mentions AirHelp, a company that assists travelers in securing compensation for flight disruptions and advocates for passenger rights. Understanding these strategies can help travelers minimize inconvenience.
